findings.location: Overview (final sentence) vs. Stage 2 — Transformation (first sentence)
findings.description: Direct logical contradiction: the Overview states all pipeline stages run in parallel, but Stage 2 explicitly states a sequential dependency — Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins. These statements are mutually exclusive.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.' If Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3, the stages cannot all be running in parallel.
findings.suggestion: Correct the Overview to accurately describe the execution model. If the architecture has sequential dependencies between stages, remove 'All pipeline stages run in parallel.' A more accurate statement would be: 'Stages within the pipeline are designed for independent scalability; Stage 2 completes before Stage 3 begins.'

findings.description: The document references 'Section 6' for security considerations, but no Section 6 exists anywhere in the document. The document has five sections: Overview, Architecture Components (with 4 stages), Performance Characteristics, Operational Notes, and Conclusions. None covers security.
findings.evidence: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' Document sections enumerated: (1) Overview, (2) Architecture Components, (3) Performance Characteristics, (4) Operational Notes, (5) Conclusions. No Section 6 exists.
findings.suggestion: Either add a Section 6 — Security Considerations covering authentication, authorization, encryption in transit, and data access controls; or remove the reference and integrate security details inline.

findings.description: The claim that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB is factually backwards. Streaming has lower overhead for small, continuous data. Batch processing's efficiency advantage emerges for large datasets where per-job setup costs are amortized. For files under 10MB, streaming typically outperforms batch.
findings.evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' No benchmark source, methodology, or test conditions are cited. The directional claim contradicts established understanding of batch vs. streaming performance trade-offs for small files.
findings.suggestion: Correct the directional claim: streaming is typically more efficient for small files (under ~100MB); batch processing excels for large datasets. Corrected: 'Benchmarks show that streaming is 3× faster than batch processing for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, batch processing is preferred.' If proprietary benchmarks genuinely show the original result, add source and methodology.

findings.description: The claim that the in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% is an extraordinary unsupported claim. A 99.9% reduction implies a 1000× improvement with no baseline, methodology, or test conditions cited.
findings.evidence: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' No measurement source, baseline latency figure, test conditions, or methodology is provided. The claim is presented as a measured fact without supporting evidence.
findings.suggestion: Remove the specific unsupported percentage. Reframe as a design characteristic: 'The in-memory delivery cache significantly reduces end-to-end latency compared to unbuffered writes by batching warehouse write operations.' If a real benchmark supports this figure, cite the source and baseline.

findings.description: The 'standard retry policy' is referenced but never defined anywhere in the document. A final architecture brief must define operational policies, not silently assume shared knowledge. Compounding the inconsistency: Stage 3 defines retry behavior explicitly (3 attempts) while Stage 2 defers to this undefined policy.
findings.evidence: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No retry policy definition, description, or link to an external specification appears anywhere in the document. Stage 3 by contrast specifies: 'the batch is requeued for up to 3 retry attempts.'
findings.suggestion: Define the retry policy inline. Example: 'Transformation applies exponential backoff retry on transient failures: up to 3 attempts with delays of 1s, 2s, and 4s.' Alternatively, define it in a dedicated Operations section and reference that definition.

findings.description: 'Large files are handled separately' — 'large' is never defined. The threshold determining which files take the overflow path vs. the primary transformation path is unspecified, making the operational boundary ambiguous.
findings.evidence: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' No size threshold or definition of 'large' appears anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Define the threshold. Example: 'Files exceeding 100MB are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' Place the threshold in configuration if it is environment-dependent, but document the default.

findings.description: 'Pipeline' and 'workflow' are used as if interchangeable but are never distinguished. In the Conclusions they appear as apparently distinct concepts in the same sentence; in Operational Notes they are coupled together without clarification.
findings.evidence: Operational Notes: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.' Conclusions: 'Future work includes adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.' If 'workflow' is distinct from 'pipeline,' it is never defined. If they are synonyms, using both creates needless ambiguity.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on 'pipeline' throughout, as it is the document's primary subject. In the Conclusions, rewrite as: 'Future work includes adding a real-time streaming pipeline alongside the existing batch pipeline.' In Operational Notes: 'The pipeline is monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.'

findings.description: 'Authentication is used' specifies that authentication occurs but provides no actionable specification of the authentication type or mechanism. For a final architecture brief, this is insufficient.
findings.evidence: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' No authentication type (API key, OAuth 2.0, mTLS, JWT, etc.) is specified anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Specify the authentication mechanism. Example: 'API key authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' Use the actual mechanism deployed in the system.

findings.description: The acronym ETL is used on first occurrence without expansion. For a final deliverable architecture brief, acronyms should be expanded on first use.
findings.evidence: 'an internal ETL system' — ETL is not expanded at its first occurrence. ETL = Extract, Transform, Load.
findings.suggestion: Change to: 'an internal ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) system' on first use.
